subject: Mathematics
contextNote: Sec 3 Express Math, 24 students. You modelled x² + 5x + 6 = (x+2)(x+3) on the board, showing how to find factor pairs that multiply to 6 and add to 5. You set 4 practice questions with increasing difficulty. Most students finished all 4 questions in under 12 minutes.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: All 4 correct in 8 minutes. Now doodling in the margin, occasionally glancing at the clock.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: All 4 correct, answers only. No working, no justification. Staring at the ceiling.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Q1-3 correct but took 15 minutes. Stuck on Q4, erasing repeatedly.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Q1 has factors written: (x+2)(x+3). Crossed out. Tried (x+1)(x+6). Also crossed out.

ep1.situation: 25 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the pattern is stark. Rajan finished all 4 questions in 8 minutes and is now doodling geometric patterns in the margin of his page, occasionally glancing at the clock. His answers are all correct: (x+2)(x+3), (x+1)(x+12), (x+3)(x+4), (x-2)(x-6). No working shown, just the final factored forms. Nur Atiqah also finished quickly — all 4 answers correct, written as bare factor pairs with no working and no justification. She is staring at the ceiling, visibly disengaged. Wei Jie is still working on Q4. His first three answers are correct, but Q4 (x² - 8x + 12) has him stuck. His page shows multiple erasures and he keeps writing and crossing out factor pairs. Siti has only attempted Q1. She has written (x+2)(x+3), crossed it out, then tried (x+1)(x+6), also crossed out. Her page is a mess of eraser marks and she looks frustrated. A quick scan of the class: 16 of 24 students have finished all 4 questions and are waiting. 5 students are still working on Q3 or Q4. 3 students, including Siti, have only completed Q1 or Q2. The fast finishers are done, but their pages show answers only — no justification, no verification, no reasoning. You have about 18 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: Most students have correct answers and finished quickly. But almost no one has written any justification for why their factors are correct, and no one has verified their answers by expanding. The speed suggests procedural fluency, but the lack of working suggests thin reasoning. You cannot tell whether students understand why factorisation works or are just pattern-matching.

choices.label: Run a Two-Step MCQ
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a new question on the board: x² + 7x + 12. Ask two questions: (1) What are the factors? Write your answer. (2) Verify your answer is correct by expanding the factors. Show your working. Ask all students to write both answers on mini whiteboards and hold them up simultaneously. Use the pattern of responses to see who can factorise, who can verify, and who can do both. If most students cannot verify, you will reteach the expansion check. If most can verify, you will move to extension.
choices.tradeoff: You get precise, whole-class evidence of who can verify their factorisation and who is just guessing or pattern-matching. The two-step structure reveals whether speed equals understanding. But it costs 3-4 minutes and interrupts the students who are still working on the original questions. Rajan and Nur, who are already disengaged, may resent being stopped for a task they find trivial.

choices.label: Push fast finishers to justify and prove
choices.elaboration: Distribute two differentiated task cards without stopping the class. Fast finishers like Rajan and Nur receive an extension card: "Choose one of your answers. Write: (1) Why are these the correct factors? (2) Prove that no other factor pair works by testing an alternate pair and showing it fails." Students still working like Wei Jie and Siti receive a prompt card: "Check your factor pair: Do they multiply to give the constant term? Do they add to give the coefficient of x? Expand your factors to verify." All versions require written justification.
choices.tradeoff: Challenges the fast finishers with proof-level reasoning (Tomlinson: teach up, not down) while supporting the middle group with verification prompts. Keeps everyone working without stopping the class. But you lose whole-class evidence of understanding, and the extension task may be too cognitively demanding for students who have only done procedural practice. Rajan may engage, but Nur may ignore the card and continue disengaging.

choices.label: Accountable Talk gallery walk
choices.elaboration: Select 4 student solutions from the completed work — choose a mix of answer-only, partial working, and full justification if any exist. Post them anonymously on the board, labeled Solution A, B, C, D. Assign students to groups of 3-4 and ask them to circulate and critique each solution using the prompt: "Is this solution complete? What is correct? What is missing? How do you know the factors are right?" After 4 minutes, use an IRF probe with the whole class: select one solution and ask students to build a complete critique together.
choices.tradeoff: Gets everyone moving and reasoning publicly. The Accountable Talk structure forces students to make claims and justify them, not just share answers. The gallery walk activates the fast finishers by giving them a reasoning task. But if all the posted solutions are superficial (answer-only), the critique will stay superficial too. Students may just say "it is correct" without explaining why. Siti, who is still stuck on Q1, may not have the confidence to critique others' work.

A.consequence: The whiteboards go up and the results are revealing. 18 of 24 students wrote the correct factors: (x+3)(x+4). But only 9 of 24 showed the expansion verification: (x+3)(x+4) = x² + 4x + 3x + 12 = x² + 7x + 12 ✓. The other 9 who got the factors right left the verification blank or wrote "correct" without showing working. Nur got the factors right but her expansion has an error: she wrote (x+3)(x+4) = x² + 7x + 16, forgetting to multiply the constants. Rajan got both parts right and when you ask him to explain, he says: "3 and 4 multiply to 12 and add to 7. I check by expanding: x² + 3x + 4x + 12." Wei Jie got the factors wrong — he wrote (x+2)(x+5), which multiply to 10, not 12. Siti left both parts blank. The pattern is now clear: procedural fluency is strong (most students can find factors quickly), but verification is weak. Many students cannot or do not check their work by expanding. You have 16 minutes remaining.

choices.label: Reteach the verification step for the whole class
choices.elaboration: Address the verification gap directly with the whole class. Write the correct factorisation on the board: (x+3)(x+4). Model the expansion step by step using the distributive property: (x+3)(x+4) = x(x+4) + 3(x+4) = x² + 4x + 3x + 12 = x² + 7x + 12 ✓. Emphasize: "Verification is not optional — it is how you know your answer is correct." Then give fast finishers an extension: "Try an alternate factor pair like (x+2)(x+6). Expand it and show why it does not work." Ask all other students to verify their original 4 answers using the expansion method.
choices.tradeoff: This is process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you are teaching the verification procedure explicitly and modeling it step by step. It will reach the 15 students who could not verify. The extension for fast finishers adds cognitive challenge. But you are reteaching Rajan something he already knows, which may disengage him further. Siti is likely to copy the board without building genuine understanding, and you will not have time to check her work individually.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) peer feedback on verifications
choices.elaboration: Use the MCQ evidence to form pairs: students who verified correctly (like Rajan) are paired with students who got the factors right but could not verify (like Nur). Ask pairs to exchange their MCQ whiteboards. The stronger student gives AQS feedback on their partner's verification attempt: Affirm what the factors got right ("You found the correct factors"), Question the verification step ("Why did you not expand to check?" or "Your expansion has an error — can you find it?"), Suggest how to verify ("Expand using the distributive property: (x+3)(x+4) = x² + 7x + 12"). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS feedback with the class.
choices.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback builds self-regulation and gives Rajan a cognitively demanding task — explaining verification is harder than just doing it. The structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. But the quality of peer explanation varies. Some students like Rajan can explain clearly; others may just give the answer without the reasoning. If Siti is paired with a student who cannot explain well, she may copy the procedure without understanding it. Fast finishers may disengage if their partner has nothing to show.

B.consequence: The extension cards go out and the room shifts into differentiated work. Rajan picks up the extension card and reads it. He selects his answer to Q2: (x+1)(x+12). He writes: "These are correct because 1 × 12 = 12 and 1 + 12 = 13. No other pair works. Try (x+2)(x+6): 2 × 6 = 12 ✓ but 2 + 6 = 8 ✗. Try (x+3)(x+4): 3 × 4 = 12 ✓ but 3 + 4 = 7 ✗." His proof is elegant and complete. Nur picks up the same card, reads the first line, then sets it aside and returns to doodling. She does not engage with the extension task. Wei Jie takes the prompt card and works through Q4 again. The prompts help — he writes: "Factors of 12: 1×12, 2×6, 3×4. Which pair adds to -8? Try -2 and -6: -2 + -6 = -8 ✓. So (x-2)(x-6)." He gets it right this time. Siti takes the prompt card and stares at it. She writes "Factors of 6: 1×6, 2×3" but does not proceed further. After a minute, she glances at her neighbor's page and copies the factors (x+2)(x+3) without working through the prompt. The room is quiet and differentiated, but you have no whole-class evidence of understanding. Rajan is engaged, but Nur is disengaged. Wei Jie made progress, but Siti is copying. 17 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) chains on the proofs
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and collect the extension cards. Select Rajan and one or two other students who wrote proofs. Ask the whole class: "Why is the alternate pair (x+2)(x+6) impossible for x² + 13x + 12? What happens when you test it?" Use an IRF chain: take Rajan's response (Initiation), ask a second student to build on it (Response), ask a third student to evaluate or add to the explanation (Feedback). Write the final class-built proof on the board. Then ask all students to choose one of their original 4 answers and write a similar proof showing why an alternate pair does not work.
choices.tradeoff: The IRF chain (common in Singapore classrooms) makes proof-level reasoning visible to the whole class and builds a shared explanation that is richer than any single student would produce. It deepens reasoning for the top group and models what proof looks like. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident and have written proofs. Students like Siti, who are still at the procedural stage, may watch passively without understanding. Wei Jie may follow the logic but struggle to produce his own proof. Nur, who did not engage with the extension, has nothing to contribute.

choices.label: Self-regulation reflection: I justified, I know, I check
choices.elaboration: Stop the differentiated work and ask all students to write a three-sentence self-regulation reflection on their page: "I justified my answer by ___ . I know it is correct because ___ . I check my work by ___." Give students 3 minutes to write individually. Then ask for two volunteers to share their reflections with the class — one from the extension group and one from the prompt group. Use their responses to highlight what good justification and verification look like.
choices.tradeoff: Self-regulation reflection (Hattie & Timperley: move students toward monitoring their own learning) builds metacognitive habits and gives you individual written evidence of understanding. It forces students to articulate their reasoning, not just produce answers. But students who stayed superficial during the differentiated work will write superficial reflections. Nur, who disengaged, may write "I justified by doing the question" without real reasoning. Siti, who copied, will have nothing genuine to write. The reflection reveals the gap but does not close it.

C.consequence: The 4 solutions go up on the board. Solution A shows all 4 answers with no working. Solution B shows Q1-Q3 with factor pairs listed but no verification. Solution C shows Q1 with full working: "Factors of 6: 1×6, 2×3. Which adds to 5? 2+3=5 ✓. So (x+2)(x+3). Check: (x+2)(x+3) = x²+5x+6 ✓." Solution D shows Q1 with an error: (x+1)(x+6). Groups circulate and the room gets noisy. Rajan's group examines Solution C and says: "This one is complete — it shows the reasoning and checks the answer. The others just have answers." They write detailed critiques. Nur's group looks at Solution A and says: "It works, doesn't it? The answers are right." When you ask "How do you know?", they shrug. Wei Jie's group examines Solution D and identifies the error: "1×6 = 6 ✓ but 1+6 = 7, not 5. So this is wrong." Siti's group stands in front of the board but does not write anything. When you approach and ask "What do you notice?", one student says "Some have working, some don't" but they do not offer a critique. After 5 minutes, you call the class back. Two groups have reasoned deeply and written substantive critiques. Four groups have stayed procedural, focusing on whether the answer is right rather than whether the reasoning is visible. The room is noisy and you have heard some strong insights, but the depth is uneven. 17 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) on the weakest gallery solution
choices.elaboration: Ask the class to vote on which solution needs the most improvement. Most students vote for Solution D (the one with the error). Run a whole-class AQS feedback session: Affirm what it got right ("The student attempted to find factor pairs"), Question the error ("Why does (x+1)(x+6) not work? What went wrong?"), Suggest how to fix it ("Check: 1+6 = 7, not 5. Try the pair that adds to 5: 2+3"). Take responses from multiple students and build a complete AQS response together. Then ask all students to re-examine their own work and verify at least one of their answers by expanding.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS structure (Affirm, Question, Suggest) turns the gallery walk evidence into structured, actionable feedback that the whole class can learn from. It is concrete because it is based on real student work. It also models the kind of critique students should be doing during gallery walks. But publicly voting on the "weakest" solution and critiquing it may embarrass or shut down the student who wrote it, even if it is posted anonymously. Some students will recognize their own work. The move also shifts back to teacher-led instruction, which may feel like a step backward after the collaborative gallery work.

choices.label: Solo proof transfer: new quadratic, individual
choices.elaboration: Give a new quadratic on the board: x² + 9x + 20 = 0. Ask each student to work individually and in silence: (1) Factorise the quadratic. (2) Write why these are the correct factors. (3) Prove that no other factor pair works by testing one alternate pair and showing it fails. Collect the responses after 5 minutes and scan them to see who can reason independently and who was relying on the gallery discussion or their group.
choices.tradeoff: Individual transfer tasks are the most honest measure of understanding (AfL Triangle: evidence of where each learner is). They reveal whether the gallery walk built genuine reasoning or just social agreement. Students who can reason independently will succeed; students who were borrowing understanding from their group will struggle. But the move to solo work kills the collaborative momentum and energy from the gallery walk. Siti, who did not engage during the gallery walk, will have nothing to build on and will likely produce minimal or no work. The task is cognitively demanding and may overwhelm students who are not yet ready for proof-level reasoning.

A1.outcome: You write the expansion verification on the board step by step: (x+3)(x+4) = x(x+4) + 3(x+4) = x² + 4x + 3x + 12 = x² + 7x + 12 ✓. The room goes quiet as students watch. You emphasize: "This is how you know your answer is correct. Verification is not optional." Nur leans forward and follows the steps carefully. She looks at her MCQ whiteboard, sees her expansion error, and rewrites it correctly. Wei Jie copies the model into his notes and then attempts to verify his original Q4 answer. He expands (x-2)(x-6) = x² - 6x - 2x + 12 = x² - 8x + 12 ✓ and looks pleased. Rajan listens to the reteach with visible boredom. When you distribute the extension task (prove an alternate pair fails), he works through it mechanically: "(x+2)(x+6) = x² + 8x + 12 ✗. Does not match x² + 7x + 12." His work is correct but perfunctory — he is not challenged. Siti watches the board and copies the expansion model line by line into her notes. When you ask her to verify one of her original answers, she hesitates and does not write anything. On a follow-up check, 20 of 24 students can now verify by expanding, up from 9. The verification gap is addressed for most of the class. But Rajan is under-challenged and disengaged, and Siti is still dependent on copying rather than understanding.
A1.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with explicit modeling is efficient and reaches the 15 students who could not verify. It is process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you taught the verification procedure step by step. Nur and Wei Jie, who were in the middle group, made clear gains. But the move treats the class as uniform. Rajan, who already understood verification, is under-challenged and bored. Siti, who is still at the access stage, remains passive and dependent. The whole-class approach served the middle well but missed the students at the top and bottom.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose this path? What were you prioritizing — efficiency, coverage, or depth?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Most students can now verify, but Rajan is disengaged and Siti is copying. What does this tell you about what "finishing" a task really means?

When fast finishers are done, what are they actually ready for — and how do you design for that in advance?

A2.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's MCQ verification. Rajan looks at Nur's whiteboard and begins: "You got the factors right — (x+3)(x+4) is correct. But your expansion has an error. You wrote x² + 7x + 16, but 3 × 4 = 12, not 16. Try expanding again: (x+3)(x+4) = x² + 3x + 4x + 12 = x² + 7x + 12." Nur listens, nods, and rewrites the expansion. This time she gets it right and says "Oh, I see — I forgot to multiply the constants." Rajan then explains why verification matters: "You expand to check. If it matches the original, your factors are correct." Nur writes this down. Wei Jie is paired with another student who verified correctly. His partner gives AQS feedback: "Your factors are wrong. 2 × 5 = 10, not 12. Try finding factors of 12 that add to 7." Wei Jie works through it again and gets (x+3)(x+4). Siti is paired with a student who got the factors right but did not verify. Her partner tries to explain: "You need to expand to check" but cannot articulate how. Siti nods but does not write anything new. When you ask her "Can you show me the expansion?", she shakes her head. The room is engaged and several students are making real gains through the peer feedback. But Rajan is not stretched — explaining verification is easier than doing proof-level reasoning. Siti is still not producing independent work. Two pairs have reinforced errors because both students in the pair could not verify.
A2.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback activates self-regulation and gives Rajan a task that is more cognitively demanding than just doing the work himself. The structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Nur and Wei Jie both made gains through structured peer support. But the quality of peer explanation varies widely. Rajan can explain clearly; Siti's partner cannot. Siti is still dependent and has not moved from copying to understanding. The pairs where both students lacked verification skills reinforced the gap rather than closing it.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose peer feedback over whole-class reteaching? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made gains, but Rajan is under-challenged and Siti is still stuck. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback?

How do you ensure peer feedback is substantive rather than social? What would you need to put in place before the next lesson?

B1.outcome: You collect the extension cards and select Rajan to share his proof with the class. Rajan explains: "I chose (x+1)(x+12). These are correct because 1 × 12 = 12 and 1 + 12 = 13. To prove no other pair works, I tested (x+2)(x+6): 2 × 6 = 12 ✓ but 2 + 6 = 8 ✗. So it fails." You ask a second student to build on Rajan's explanation: "What about (x+3)(x+4)?" The student responds: "3 × 4 = 12 ✓ but 3 + 4 = 7 ✗. Also fails." A third student evaluates: "So only (x+1)(x+12) works because it is the only pair that multiplies to 12 and adds to 13." The IRF chain produces a clear, class-built proof that is more detailed than any single student would have given. You write the final explanation on the board. Wei Jie listens carefully and nods — he follows the logic. Nur watches but does not contribute. When you ask her "Does this make sense?", she says "Yes" but her tone is uncertain. Siti watches the exchange but when you glance at her, she looks down at her page. The IRF chain has made proof-level reasoning visible, but it is driven by the top students. The majority of the class are observers rather than participants. Rajan is engaged, but Nur and Siti are passive. 16 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: The IRF chain makes proof-level reasoning visible and accessible to the whole class. It builds a shared explanation that is richer and more robust than a teacher-led explanation because it is constructed from student reasoning. Wei Jie, who is in the middle group, followed the logic and learned what proof looks like. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident and have written proofs. Nur and Siti, who did not engage with the extension task, have nothing to contribute and remain passive observers. The reasoning belongs to Rajan and the few students who speak, not to everyone.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use IRF chains to make the proofs public? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The proof logic is now visible, but only a few students contributed. What does this tell you about who does the thinking in an IRF chain?

How do you design whole-class discussion so that the reasoning belongs to everyone, not just the students who speak?

B2.outcome: You ask all students to write their three-sentence self-regulation reflections. The room goes quiet. Rajan writes: "I justified my answer by testing factor pairs to see which multiply to the constant and add to the coefficient. I know it is correct because I proved that alternate pairs fail. I check by expanding the factors and verifying they match the original quadratic." His reflection is detailed, precise, and shows deep metacognitive awareness. Nur writes: "I justified by finding the factors. I know it is correct because the factors multiply to 6 and add to 5. I check by... I don't know." Her reflection reveals that she can execute the procedure but cannot articulate how to verify. Wei Jie writes: "I justified by doing the question. I know because I got the right answer. I check by looking at it again." His reflection is procedurally compliant but shows no genuine reasoning — he is describing what he did, not why it works. Siti writes one sentence: "I justified by trying different factors." Then she stops. Her reflection is incomplete and reveals that she has no clear justification process. You collect the responses and scan them. The self-regulation task has given you honest individual evidence, but it has also revealed that the extension task did not build deep understanding for most students. Procedural fluency is masking shallow reasoning.
B2.tradeoff: Self-regulation reflection (Hattie & Timperley: move students toward self-monitoring) builds metacognitive habits and gives you clean individual evidence of understanding. Rajan's reflection shows he has internalized proof-level reasoning. But Nur, Wei Jie, and Siti's reflections reveal that the differentiated work did not reach them. Nur stayed procedural, Wei Jie stayed superficial, and Siti disengaged. The reflection exposes the gap but does not close it. You now have evidence of who understands and who does not, but you have less time to respond.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose self-regulation reflection after differentiated work? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan shows deep understanding, but most students stayed procedural. What does this tell you about the difference between doing a task and understanding it?

How do you design extension tasks that build genuine reasoning, not just procedural compliance?

C1.outcome: The class votes and Solution D (the one with the error) receives the most votes. You run a whole-class AQS session. Several students affirm: "The student tried to find factor pairs." You write this on the board. Then you ask: "What should we question?" Nur raises her hand and says: "The factors are wrong. 1 + 6 = 7, not 5." You write: "Question: Why does (x+1)(x+6) not work?" Then you ask: "What would you suggest?" Wei Jie says: "Try the pair that adds to 5. That is 2 + 3." You write: "Suggest: Use (x+2)(x+3) because 2 × 3 = 6 and 2 + 3 = 5." The AQS feedback is now complete and visible on the board. You ask all students to verify at least one of their original answers by expanding. Nur expands her Q1 answer and gets it right this time. Wei Jie verifies his Q4 answer successfully. Rajan was already correct and does not need to verify, but he listens to the AQS process with mild interest. Siti copies the AQS feedback from the board into her notes but when you ask her to verify her Q1 answer, she hesitates and does not write anything. The student whose Solution D was critiqued looks uncomfortable — you notice them glancing around the room to see if anyone recognizes their work. The AQS process has turned the gallery walk into a structured teachable moment, but it has also created a moment of public exposure. 16 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS feedback structure is concrete and actionable because it is based on real student work from the gallery walk. It models the kind of critique students should be doing and makes the error correction process transparent. Nur and Wei Jie, who were in the middle group, made clear gains. But the student whose work was critiqued feels exposed, even with anonymous posting. In a class of 24, students often recognize their own work. Siti is still copying rather than reasoning — the AQS process did not move her from dependence to understanding. Rajan is under-challenged.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to make the error public and use AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn from this?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie self-corrected, but one student felt exposed and Siti is still copying. What does this tell you about using student work publicly?

How do you build a classroom culture where errors are learning opportunities rather than sources of embarrassment?

C2.outcome: You write the new quadratic on the board: x² + 9x + 20 = 0. Students work individually and in silence. Rajan completes it in under 2 minutes. He writes: "(x+4)(x+5). Correct because 4 × 5 = 20 and 4 + 5 = 9. Alternate pair (x+2)(x+10): 2 × 10 = 20 ✓ but 2 + 10 = 12 ✗. Fails." His proof is complete and shows deep understanding. Nur factorises correctly: (x+4)(x+5). She writes: "4 × 5 = 20 and 4 + 5 = 9." But her proof is incomplete — she does not test an alternate pair or explain why no other pair works. Wei Jie gets the factors right this time: (x+4)(x+5). But he cannot write the proof. His page says: "I think these are right because they work." He has not internalized what proof means. Siti gets the factors wrong. She writes (x+2)(x+10) and leaves the proof section blank. The individual responses give you clean evidence of where each student is. Rajan can reason independently at proof level. Nur can factorise but cannot prove. Wei Jie can factorise but cannot justify. Siti cannot factorise reliably. The move to solo work has revealed that the gallery discussion did not build deep understanding for most students. The energy and momentum from the collaborative work are gone.
C2.tradeoff: Individual transfer tasks are the most honest measure of understanding (AfL Triangle: evidence of where each learner is). They reveal whether the gallery walk built genuine reasoning or just social agreement. Rajan demonstrated deep independent reasoning. But Nur, Wei Jie, and Siti struggled, which suggests the gallery walk was more social than cognitive for them. You now have clear evidence of the gap, but the move to solo work has killed the collaborative energy. Siti, who did not engage during the gallery walk, has nothing to build on and produced minimal work.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from collaborative gallery work to solo transfer? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan succeeded, but most students struggled. What does this tell you about when collaborative work builds real learning versus borrowed understanding?

What would need to be different about the gallery walk for the solo transfer to go better?
